title: Leaf impression tile set in air-dry clay
subtitle: A set of three tiles each with a different plant impression
excerpt: A leaf impression tile set uses different leaves pressed into three matching clay slabs to create a botanical grouping. The tiles are cut to the same size and hung together. The leaf impression is pressed at the soft stage immediately after rolling, which gives the clearest vein detail. No kiln or wheel is needed.
sourceNotes: Leaf impression tile technique. Technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: A flat sheet of clay rolled to an even thickness. The base form for these tiles.
Fully air-dried clay. The tiles must be bone-dry before sealing and hanging.

aliases: air dry clay leaf impression tiles

content.text: , each with a different leaf pressed into it, make a botanical set that reads as a series when hung together. The tiles should be the same size and cut from the same rolled slab to ensure uniform thickness and colour. The interest comes from the variety of the leaf impressions, not from the form of the tiles themselves.

attrs.title: Leaf choice',
attrs.body: Choose three leaves with visibly different vein structures for maximum contrast across the set. A fern frond, a large round hosta leaf, and a narrow oak or maple leaf make a varied trio. Press each leaf underside-down onto the clay: the vein pattern is most pronounced on the underside.

items.name: Three fresh leaves with prominent veins
Pottery rolling pin and 6 mm guide rails

content.text: Step 1: Roll a single large slab

Roll all 600 g of clay into a single slab 6 mm thick. Rolling from one block ensures all three tiles are the same thickness, colour, and surface texture. A slab at least 50 cm long gives enough room to cut three tiles each 15 x 12 cm.

Place each leaf underside-down in its tile area. Roll once firmly over each leaf with the rolling pin to press the vein pattern into the clay. Peel each leaf away slowly from one corner. The impression should be clearly visible in the clay. Work quickly on all three leaves while the clay is still fresh.

Cut three identical rectangles from the leaf-impressed areas, avoiding any impressed area near the edges of the slab. Use the fettling knife against a ruler for straight cuts. Each tile should be the same size.

Pierce two holes in each tile near the top edge, 3 cm apart and 1.5 cm from the top. All three tiles should have their holes in the same position so they hang at the same height when mounted side by side.
Dry all three tiles between two boards with a light weight on top, as described in the drying-flat technique, for the first four hours. Allow to reach
in open air over 24 to 48 hours. Seal with two coats of PVA diluted 1:1. Thread cord through the holes and tie.
Paint the recessed vein lines with a dark acrylic paint before sealing, leaving the raised areas the natural clay colour.
Cut the tiles to different shapes, rectangle, oval, hexagon, for a less uniform set.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Problems when making the leaf impression tile set.

items.symptom: Three tiles vary in thickness after cutting
items.cause: Clay was rolled in two batches rather than one large slab.
items.fix: Always roll all clay for a matched set from a single slab in one session. Re-roll the thicker piece carefully, removing the guide rails and rolling by hand until it matches.

items.symptom: Leaf impressions are fainter on one tile than the others
items.cause: Less rolling pressure applied over that leaf.
items.fix: Roll firmly and evenly across the full area of each leaf. Check the impression before removing the leaf and re-roll if faint areas remain.
